concepts.title: Data, Tally Marks and Frequency
concepts.definition: Any collection of facts, numbers, measures, observations or other descriptions of things that convey information about those things is called data. To organise data, tools like tally marks (grouped in fives as ||||) and frequency tables are used to record the count of occurrences.
examples: Navya and Naresh collected a list of favorite games (Cricket, Hockey, Kabaddi, Football, etc.) from their classmates to find the most popular one.
Shri Nilesh used tally marks to record students' preferences for sweets: Jalebi (6), Gulab jamun (9), Gujiya (13), Barfi (3), and Rasgulla (7).
Sushri Sandhya arranged shoe sizes (3, 4, 5, 6, 7) in ascending order to easily count frequencies and find the largest/smallest sizes.
Recording the count of different trees (Peepal, Neem, etc.) seen on the way to school in a frequency table.
Counting the frequency of specific letters like 'c', 'e', 'i', 'r', 'x' in a news article to observe patterns.

concepts.title: Pictographs and Scale
concepts.definition: A pictograph represents data through pictures of objects. It helps answer questions and make inferences about data with just a quick glance. A scale or key (e.g., one symbol = 10 children) is added to show what each symbol or picture represents, especially when handling large amounts of data.
formulae: Total Value = Number of symbols × Value per symbol
examples: A pictograph of travel modes where one symbol (©) represents 1 student: School bus (11), Walking (7), Cycle (3), Public bus (5), Private car (4).
Nand Kishor's pictograph for hours of sleep using a symbol for 10 children: 'Always' (50 children), 'Sometimes' (25 children), 'Never' (40 children).
Lakhanpal and his friends used ⊙ to represent 1 or 5 students absent/present in different classes.
Magan Bhai's kite sales pictograph where one symbol ⊕ represents 100 kites sold to shopkeepers (e.g., Poonam Ben purchased 7 symbols = 700 kites).
Library books borrowed in a week (Monday to Saturday) represented by book symbols.

concepts.title: Bar Graphs (Interpretation)
concepts.definition: Bar graphs display data using rectangular bars of uniform width with lengths or heights proportional to the frequencies or values they represent. Bars are drawn horizontally or vertically with equal spacing between them to indicate they are free-standing categories.
formulae: The length/height of each bar represents the given frequency.
examples: A bar graph showing the increasing number of endangered species (mammals, birds, reptiles, etc.) over time.
Lakhanpal's bar graph of students absent in each class highlights the class with the maximum absentees (Class 8) and full attendance (Class 5).
A traffic police study of vehicular traffic at a crossing showing 1200 vehicles during the peak hour (7-8 a.m.) using a scale of 1 unit = 100 vehicles.
Population of India in each decade over 50 years, where 1 unit length represents 10 crores.

concepts.title: Drawing a Bar Graph and Choosing a Scale
concepts.definition: Drawing a bar graph requires horizontal and vertical axes, labeling categories, and choosing a suitable scale (e.g., 1 unit length = 10 runs or ₹200) based on the minimum and maximum frequencies so the graph fits nicely and is visually appealing.
formulae: Scale choice = (Maximum frequency) / (Available space or convenient units)
examples: Drawing a bar graph for Smriti's scores (80, 50, 10, 100, 90, 0, 90, 50) using a scale where 1 unit length = 10 runs.
Imran's family's monthly expenditure bar graph uses a scale of 1 unit length = ₹200 to represent items like House Rent (15 units) and Food (17 units).
A bar graph illustrating favorite free-time activities (Playing, Reading, Watching TV, etc.) for 120 students using a scale of 1 unit = 5 students.
Recording and graphing Jaspreet Bumrah's wickets in 30 matches, or the number of girl students per class (1 unit = 4 girls).
Graphing the increase in Mudhol Hounds (dogs) in Karnataka villages using a scale of 1 symbol = 6 dogs.

concepts.title: Artistic Considerations and Infographics
concepts.definition: Data presentations can be enhanced through artistic and aesthetic choices like color, orientation (vertical column graphs for heights vs horizontal graphs for lengths), and infographics. Infographics (information graphics) use engaging visuals to communicate information quickly, though accuracy must be prioritized to avoid misleading the audience.
examples: Representing mountain heights with vertical 'column graphs' grows upward like mountains, making comparison intuitive.
An infographic comparing tallest mountains (Everest, etc.) using triangle shapes where taller triangles are also wider, which can mislead regarding actual width.
Creating a realistic-looking imaginary mountain range to visually ground the comparison of peak heights.
Choosing between vertical and horizontal bars based on whether a feature is vertical (height) or horizontal (river length).

concepts.title: Fractional Units and Unit Fractions (Equal Shares)
concepts.definition: When one unit is divided into several equal parts, each part is called a fractional unit. These are also known as 'unit fractions' (e.g., 1/2, 1/3, 1/4). Fractions represent how much each share is when a whole number of things is shared equally.
examples: When 1 roti is divided equally between 2 children, each gets 1/2 roti.
Three guavas together weigh 1 kg; if roughly the same size, each weighs 1/3 kg.
A wholesale merchant packs 1 kg of rice into four packets; each weighs 1/4 kg.
Four friends sharing 3 glasses of sugarcane juice equally; each drinks 3/4 glass.
A big fish weighs 1/2 kg and a small one weighs 1/4 kg; together they weigh 3/4 kg.

concepts.title: Numerator, Denominator and Parts of a Whole
concepts.definition: In a fraction like 5/6, 5 is the numerator (top number) and 6 is the denominator (bottom number). The denominator indicates the total number of equal parts the whole is divided into, while the numerator is the number of such parts being considered.
examples: In the fraction 5/6, 5 is the numerator and 6 is the denominator.
A whole chikki broken into 6 equal pieces (1/6 each) or 12 equal pieces (1/12 each) in different shapes but same size.
Visualizing 3/4 as '3 times 1/4', which shows 3 fractional units of size 1/4.

concepts.title: Measuring and Folding (Fractional Units)
concepts.definition: Fractional quantities can be measured by collecting together fractional units. Using a paper strip as one unit, folding it into equal parts creates fractional units (1/2, 1/4, 1/8) that can be used for measurement.
formulae: Quantity = Number of fractional units × Size of fractional unit
examples: Fold a paper strip into two equal parts (1/2), then four (1/4), then eight (1/8) to measure lengths.
Representing 5 times 1/4 of a roti as 5/4, or 9 times 1/4 as 9/4.
Matching fractional units like 1/6 or 1/4 with corresponding visual shapes/pictures.

concepts.title: Fractions on the Number Line (Uncountable Fractions)
concepts.definition: Every fraction has a unique point associated with it on the number line. Fractions between 0 and 1 are less than 1 unit, while those where the numerator is larger than the denominator are more than 1 unit.
examples: Marking unit fractions like 1/10 and 3/10 or non-unit fractions like 4/5 on a number line.
Showing length increments of 1/2, 1/3, 1/5, or 1/8 units by dividing the distance between 0 and 1.
Identifying that there are an uncountable number of fractions between 0 and 1.

concepts.title: Mixed Fractions and Fractions Greater than One
concepts.definition: A mixed fraction (mixed number) contains a whole number part and a fractional part less than 1. Fractions where the numerator is larger than the denominator are greater than 1 unit.
formulae: Mixed Fraction = Whole number + Proper Fraction
examples: Converting 5/2 to 2 + 1/2, written as the mixed number 2 1/2.
The fraction 8/3 is written as 2 2/3 (two and two thirds).
Converting improper fractions like 9/2, 9/5, 21/19, and 19/6 to mixed fractions.
Converting mixed numbers like 3 1/4 back to improper fractions: (3 × 4) + 1 = 13/4.

concepts.title: Equivalent Fractions and Simplest Form (Lowest Terms)
concepts.definition: Fractions that denote the same length or share are equivalent fractions. A fraction is in simplest form (lowest terms) if the numerator and denominator have no common factor except 1.
formulae: Equivalent Fraction = (Numerator × n) / (Denominator × n) where n is non-zero.
examples: Using a fraction wall to see that 1/2 = 2/4 = 4/8 or 1/3 = 2/6.
Reducing 16/20 to 4/5 by dividing both by 4 (the common factor).
Expressing 36/60 in lowest terms as 3/5 by dividing by common factors (2, 2, 3) or directly by 12.
Finding equivalent fractions for 2/6 like 1/3 or 3/9.

concepts.title: Comparing Fractions (Common Fractional Units)
concepts.definition: To compare fractions, convert them to equivalent fractions with the same denominator (common fractional unit) and then compare numerators. The larger numerator indicates a larger share.
formulae: To compare a/b and c/d, compare (a×d)/(b×d) and (c×b)/(d×b).
examples: Comparing 1/2 and 5/8 by converting 1/2 to 4/8 (4/8 < 5/8).
Deciding between 3 glasses for 4 children (3/4) and 7 glasses for 10 children (7/10) by using common denominator 40 (30/40 > 28/40).
Comparing 4/7 and 4/8: when sharing 4 units, fewer children (7) get a larger share than 8 children.
Arranging fractions like (7/10, 11/15, 2/5) in ascending or descending order.

concepts.title: Addition of Fractions (Brahmagupta’s Method)
concepts.definition: Brahmagupta's method (628 CE) involves finding equivalent fractions with a common fractional unit (denominator), then adding the numerators while keeping the same denominator.
formulae: a/b + c/d = (ad + bc) / bd
examples: Adding 1/4 + 1/3 by converting to 3/12 + 4/12 = 7/12.
Rahim mixing paint: 2/3 liters yellow + 3/4 liters blue = 17/12 = 1 5/12 liters green paint.
Geeta (2/5 m) and Shamim (3/4 m) buying lace for a 1-meter perimeter: 2/5 + 3/4 = 23/20 = 1 3/20 m (sufficient).

concepts.title: Subtraction of Fractions (Brahmagupta’s Method)
concepts.definition: Subtracting fractions using Brahmagupta's method requires converting them to a common denominator and then taking the difference of the numerators.
formulae: a/b - c/d = (ad - bc) / bd
examples: Subtracting 4/7 from 6/7 = 2/7.
Jaya's school walk: 7/10 km total - 1/2 km by auto = 7/10 - 5/10 = 2/10 = 1/5 km.
Comparing time taken to round a park: Jeevika (10/3 min) vs Namit (13/4 min); difference is 1/12 min.

concepts.title: History: Bhinna, Bhaga and Ancient Notation
concepts.definition: Fractions were called 'bhinna' (broken), 'bhaga' or 'ansha' (part) in ancient India. Rules were codified by Brahmagupta and notation evolved from the Bakshali manuscript through Aryabhata and Sridharacharya.
examples: Vedic times: Rig Veda mentions 'tri-pada' for 3/4.
Writing 1/2 as a numerator above a denominator without a bar (Bakshali manuscript).
Al-Hassar (12th century Moroccan) introducing the horizontal line segment we use today.
Egyptian fractions as sums of different fractional units: 1 = 1/2 + 1/3 + 1/6 (three different units).

concepts.title: Point (Precise Location)
concepts.definition: A point determines a precise location in space. It has no length, breadth, or height. It is typically represented by a tiny dot and denoted by a single capital letter (e.g., Point P).
examples: The tip of a compass, the sharpened end of a pencil, and the pointed end of a needle are models for a point.
Dots represent precise locations and must be imagined to be invisibly thin.
Distinguishing three points by naming them Z, P, and T.

concepts.title: Line Segment (Shortest Path)
concepts.definition: A line segment corresponds to the shortest distance between two points, which are called its end points. A line segment from A to B is denoted as AB or BA.
examples: Folding a piece of paper and unfolding it creates a crease that gives the idea of a line segment.
Connecting points A and B by various routes; the shortest path is the line segment AB.
Identifying line segments in a figure, such as LM, MP, PQ, and QR.

concepts.title: Line (Indefinite Extension)
concepts.definition: A line is a line segment extended indefinitely in both directions. It has no end points. A line through points A and B is written as ↔AB.
formulae: Any two points determine a unique line passing through them.
examples: Imagine line segment AB extended beyond A and B in both directions without any end.
A line can be denoted by a single small letter like l or m.
Drawing many lines through a single point Rihan marked vs. one line through two points Sheetal marked.

concepts.title: Ray (One-Way Extension)
concepts.definition: A ray is a portion of a line that starts at one point (called the starting point or initial point) and goes on endlessly in one direction.
examples: Beam of light from a lighthouse, rays from a torch, or sun rays.
Ray OA starts at O and passes through A; it cannot be written as AO because the starting point is fixed.
Rays from point T in multiple directions like TA, TB, and TN.

concepts.title: Angle (Vertex and Arms)
concepts.definition: An angle is formed by two rays having a common starting point called the vertex. The rays are called the arms of the angle. An angle is named using the vertex in the middle (e.g., ∠DBE).
formulae: Angle size = amount of rotation or turn needed about the vertex.
examples: The point B where rays BD and BE meet is the vertex; BD and BE are the arms.
Opening the cover of a book at different amounts of rotation creates angles of different sizes.
Identifying vertex and arms in a pair of scissors, a compass, or a divider.

concepts.title: Comparing Angles (Superimposition and Rotation)
concepts.definition: The size of an angle is determined by the amount of rotation, not the length of its arms. Angles can be compared by superimposition (overlapping vertices and arms) or by tracing and measuring.
examples: Comparing the gap in animals' mouths as they open: wider opening means a larger angle.
Using a transparent circle to check rotation: placing the center on the vertex to mark arm positions.
Evaluating which angle is greater among ∠AOB and ∠XOY by superimposing them.

concepts.title: Special Types: Straight and Right Angles
concepts.definition: A straight angle is a half turn ($180°$) where the arms lie in a straight line. A right angle is exactly half of a straight angle ($90°$), resembling an 'L' shape. Lines meeting at right angles are perpendicular.
formulae: 1 Straight Angle = 2 Right Angles
Right Angle = 1/4 of a full turn
examples: A full turn of a notebook cover vs. a half turn (straight angle) when lying flat.
Perpendicular creases formed by folding paper twice; creating four right angles around a point.
Finding right angles in classroom windows and grid points.

concepts.title: Classifying Angles: Acute, Obtuse, and Reflex
concepts.definition: Angles are classified by size: Acute (less than $90°$), Right ($90°$), Obtuse (between $90°$ and $180°$), and Reflex (between $180°$ and $360°$).
formulae: 0° < Acute < 90°
90° < Obtuse < 180°
examples: Acute means 'sharp' and Obtuse means 'blunt'; these reflect the visual sharpness of the corner.
Classifying angles like ∠PTR ($30°$, acute) and ∠WTP ($258°$, reflex).
Identifying patterns of acute angles in nested triangle figures.

concepts.title: Measuring Angles in Degrees (Protractor)
concepts.definition: The measure of an angle is the number of 1-degree ($1°$) unit parts it contains. A full circle is divided into 360 equal parts. A protractor is a tool divided into 180 (semicircle) or 360 (full circle) degree marks.
formulae: Full Turn = 360°
Half Turn (Straight) = 180°
Quarter Turn (Right) = 90°
examples: 360 is used because it is divisible by all numbers up to 10 except 7, and matches many ancient calendar systems.
Rigveda Reference: A wheel with 360 spokes (Verse 1.164.48).
Making a handmade protractor by folding a circular paper into halves, quarters, and eighths ($45°$, $22.5°$).

concepts.title: Angles in Real Life and Clocks
concepts.definition: Angles are used to describe rotation and slope in daily life, such as the position of clock hands, doors, swings, and slabs.
formulae: Angle between successive numbers on a clock = 360° / 12 = 30°
examples: At 1 o'clock, the angle is $30°$; at 3 o'clock, $90°$; at 6 o'clock, $180°$.
The angle of a door: vertex at the hinge, arms are the edges of the door and wall.
Swings and toys: steeper slopes/angles lead to higher speeds of movement.
Ashoka Chakra: 24 spokes create an angle of $15°$ between successive spokes.

concepts.title: Angle Bisector
concepts.definition: The line that divides a given angle into two equal parts is called the angle bisector. This process is called bisecting the angle.
formulae: Bisected Angle = Original Angle / 2
examples: Folding a paper protractor in half at each step is a process of bisecting angles (180 to 90, 90 to 45).
Drawing a ray OC such that ∠AOC = ∠BOC within a straight angle ∠AOB.
Finding the degree measure of parts when a 180° angle is divided into 8 equal parts (22.5°).

concepts.title: Numbers Telling Stories (Context)
concepts.definition: Numbers convey information relative to their context. For example, a number can represent a count, position, or a specific property like height relative to neighbors.
examples: Children in a park say a number representing their taller neighbors: 1 if one side is taller, 2 if both are taller, 0 if neither.
Analyzing height sequences through numbers: if heights are different, the ends cannot say '2'.
Arranging children such that the sequence of neighbors' heights is 0, 1, 2, 1, 0.

concepts.title: Supercells (Local Maxima)
concepts.definition: In a sequence or a grid, a 'supercell' is a number that is greater than all its adjacent (neighboring) cells (left, right, top, bottom).
formulae: For a 1D table with n cells: Max supercells = n/2 (if even) or (n+1)/2 (if odd, alternating).
examples: In the sequence [200, 577, 626, 345], 626 is a supercell because it's larger than 577 and 345.
In a 2D grid, 8632 is a supercell if it exceeds its neighbors 4580, 8280, 4795, and 1944.
If you exchange digits 1 and 6 in 62,871 to get 12,876, a grid can have exactly 4 supercells.
Fact: The largest number in any table will always be a supercell, while the smallest can never be one.

concepts.title: Number Line Positioning and Comparisons
concepts.definition: Placing numbers on a number line helps in visual comparison and understanding relative magnitudes of large numbers.
examples: Identifying patterns in labeling remaining positions on a scale of 1000s.
Pratibha's Comparison: Largest 4-digit from {4, 7, 3, 2} is 7432 and smallest is 2347. Difference is 5085; sum is 9779.

concepts.title: Digit Sums and Number Properties
concepts.definition: The digit sum is the total obtained by adding all digits of a number. Different numbers can share the same digit sum, aiding in pattern recognition.
examples: Komal's observation: Numbers with digit sum 14: 68 (6+8), 176 (1+7+6), 545 (5+4+5).
The smallest number with digit sum 14 is 59; the largest 5-digit number is 95000.
Calculated pattern: Sums of digits of consecutive 3-digit numbers (e.g., 345 → 12) are always multiples of 3 (6, 9, 12, 15, 18, 21, 24).

concepts.title: Digit Detectives (Occurrences and place value)
concepts.definition: Counting digit appearances across ranges follows arithmetic patterns based on units, tens, and hundreds places.
formulae: Count in 1-100 = 20 occurrences per non-zero digit.
examples: Dinesh's Count: Digit '7' appears 20 times between 1-100 and 300 times between 1-1000.
Total count of k-digit numbers: 1-digit (9), 2-digit (90), 3-digit (900), 4-digit (9000), 5-digit (90,000).

concepts.title: Palindromic numbers and patterns
concepts.definition: Palindromes are numbers that read the same backwards and forwards. Complex palindromes can be created using specific rule-based procedures.
examples: Puzzle: A 5-digit odd palindrome with 't' double of 'u' and 'h' double of 't' is 12421.
Reverse-and-Add: 76 + 67 = 143; 143 + 341 = 484 (Palindrome in 2 rounds).

concepts.title: Kaprekar Constant (The Magic of 6174)
concepts.definition: Discovered by D.R. Kaprekar in 1949, this constant is reached via a subtraction routine where digits are sorted in descending and ascending order.
formulae: Descending Digits - Ascending Digits = New Number.
examples: Starting with 6382 reaches 6174 in 3 steps: 6264 → 4176 → 6174.
The number 5683 takes 8 rounds to reach 6174.
For 3-digit numbers, the magic number that repeats is 495.

concepts.title: Clock, Calendar and Time Patterns
concepts.definition: Numbers on devices like clocks and calendars show symmetrical and order-based patterns (e.g., palindromic time).
formulae: Calendar Repeats: Every 6 years (if 1 leap year) or 5 years (if 2 leap years) in short cycles.
examples: Manish's repeating birthday: 20/12/2012; Meghana's palindromic birthday: 11/02/2011.
Palindromic Times: 10:01, 11:11, 12:21; Interval between 10:01 and 11:11 is 70 minutes.
Reuse of calendars: Jeevan's inquiry about reusing calendars after several years.

concepts.title: Number Patterns and Grids
concepts.definition: Visual patterns in number grids allow for rapid calculation and discovering structural properties of arithmetic sequences.
examples: Shortcut for summing a 4x4 grid of 32s: 32 × 16 = 512, which is faster than adding 16 individual numbers.
Building patterns that sum to a specific target (e.g., a 2x2 grid of 50s and 25s to reach 250).
Analyzing rows of 40s and 50s in classes to find total counts via multiplication.

concepts.title: The Collatz Conjecture (3n + 1)
concepts.definition: An unsolved problem (Lothar Collatz, 1937) where a sequence always reaches 1 if you: half even numbers and triple+1 odd numbers.
formulae: If n is even: n → n/2
If n is odd: n → 3n + 1
examples: Sequence starting at 12: 12, 6, 3, 10, 5, 16, 8, 4, 2, 1.
The conjecture is proved for powers of 2 (Chapter 1) but remains unsolved for others.

concepts.title: Simple Estimation in Daily Life
concepts.definition: Estimation (approximately counting) is useful when exact numbers are not required. It relies on scaling samples or using standard rates.
examples: Paromita's school estimate: 32 per section × 3 sections × 5 classes ≈ 500 students.
Sheetal's school hours check: 6 hrs/day × 200 days/yr × 8 yrs ≈ 9,600 hours (13,000 is an over-estimate).
Estimating steps based on distance/pace: From classroom to door, or school to home.

concepts.title: Winning Game Strategies (Game 21)
concepts.definition: Games like '21' have mathematical winning strategies based on reaching specific 'save' numbers to force an opponent into an unavoidable loss.
formulae: Target = N: if you can add k to previous, the winning series is N-(step+1).
examples: Rules: Adding 1, 2, or 3 to reach 21. Winning strategy involves identifying patterns the first player should say.
Variation: Adding 1-10 to reach 99. Finding out if the first or second player has the advantage.
Strategy for reaching 22 by adding 1, 2, or 3: working backwards from the goal.

concepts.title: Mathematics as the Search for Patterns
concepts.definition: Mathematics is the search for patterns and explanations for why they exist. This search combines art and science and helps propel humanity forward (e.g., gravitation, diseases).
examples: Patterns in nature and daily life: shopping, cooking, sports, and technology.
Theory of gravitation developed from patterns in the motion of stars and planets.
Diagnosing diseases through patterns in genomes.

concepts.title: Key Number Sequences
concepts.definition: The branch of Mathematics studying patterns in whole numbers is Number Theory. Sequences are ordered lists of numbers following specific rules.
examples: Triangular numbers: 1, 3, 6, 10, 15, 21, 28, 36... (Can be arranged in a triangle of dots).
Virahnka numbers (6th-8th century): 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21... (Each term is the sum of the previous two).

concepts.title: Visualising Numbers (Squares, Cubes, and Hexagons)
concepts.definition: Numbers can be represented pictorially using dot patterns to reveal their geometric properties.
formulae: n-th Square number = n × n
n-th Cube number = n × n × n
examples: Square numbers (1, 4, 9, 16...) can be arranged in a square grid of dots.
Cube numbers (1, 8, 27...) can be represented using actual 3D cubes.
Fact: 36 is 'square-triangular' as it fits both patterns (6×6 square and T_8 triangle).
Hexagonal numbers: 1, 7, 19, 37, 61, 91... (Formed by multiplication and addition relations).

concepts.title: Relationships Between Sequences
concepts.definition: Surprising relations exist where adding the terms of one sequence results in another sequence.
formulae: Sum of first n odd numbers = n².
Sum of first n counting numbers up and down = n².
Hexagonal Number = (Triangular Number × 6) + 1.
examples: Sum of first 10 odds is 100; sum of first 100 odds is 10,000.
Relation: T_n + T_{n-1} = n² (e.g., 3 + 6 = 9).
Building cubes: Adding hexagonal numbers 1, 1+7=8, 1+7+19=27 results in the cube sequence.

concepts.title: Patterns in Shapes (Regular Polygons and Graphs)
concepts.definition: Geometry studies patterns in shapes across different dimensions (1D, 2D, 3D).
examples: Regular Polygon Names: Triangle (3), Square (4), Pentagon (5), Hexagon (6), Heptagon (7), Octagon (8), Nonagon (9), Decagon (10).
In regular polygons, the number of sides always equals the number of corners.
Complete Graphs: Connecting all possible points. Number of lines follows triangular numbers (1, 3, 6, 10...).

concepts.title: Fractal Patterns (The Koch Snowflake)
concepts.definition: Complex patterns formed by simple recursive rules that add increasingly smaller details to a shape.
formulae: Koch Segment Rule: Replace each '—' with a 'speed bump' shape.
Number of segments: 3, 12, 48, 192... (3 × Powers of 4).
examples: Starting with a triangle, each side is replaced by a 'speed bump' iteratively.
As iterations increase, the line segments become tinier and tinier, forming a snowflake shape.

concepts.title: Perimeter of Polygons
concepts.definition: The distance covered along the boundary of a closed plane figure. For a polygon, it is the sum of the lengths of all its sides.
formulae: Perimeter of Rectangle = 2 × (length + breadth)
Perimeter of Square = 4 × length of a side
Perimeter of Triangle = Sum of lengths of its three sides
examples: A rectangle with length 12 cm and breadth 8 cm has a perimeter of 40 cm.
Debojeet's 1m square photo frame requires 4m of tape for its perimeter.
Akshi and Toshi's comparison: Akshi runs 1100m (5 rounds of 220m track); Toshi runs 1260m (7 rounds of 180m track).
Missing terms: If P=14 cm and breadth=2 cm, length is 5 cm; if P=20 cm for a square, side is 5 cm.
Fencing: A 150m × 120m park costs Rs 21,600 to fence at Rs 40/metre.

concepts.title: Perimeter of Regular Polygons
concepts.definition: Closed figures with all sides and all angles equal are regular polygons.
formulae: Perimeter = Number of sides × Length of one side
examples: Equilateral Triangle: 3 × side; Square: 4 × side; Regular Hexagon: 6 × side.
A 36 cm string forms a square of side 9 cm, a triangle of side 12 cm, or a hexagon of side 6 cm.
Usha covers 900m in three rounds of a 75m square park.

concepts.title: Complex Perimeter (Straight and Diagonal Units)
concepts.definition: Boundaries on a grid consist of straight (s) and diagonal (d) units. Diagonal lines (blue) are longer than straight lines (red).
formulae: Perimeter = (Count of s) + (Count of d)
examples: A triangle shape might have a perimeter like '6s + 3d' units.
Wire Puzzle: A 5cm × 3cm rectangle (P=16) is straightened and bent into a square of side 4cm.
Split and Rejoin: A 6cm × 4cm paper cut in two can form shapes with perimeters like 28cm or 22cm.

concepts.title: Area Concept and Units
concepts.definition: The amount of surface region enclosed by a closed figure, measured generally in square units (sq units).
examples: Estimation: On a grid, count full squares as 1, count > half as 1, count exactly half as 1/2, and ignore < half.
Merit of Square units: Circles cannot be packed tightly without gaps, making them inaccurate as units for area.

concepts.title: Area of Rectangles and Squares
concepts.definition: The area of a rectangle is length × breadth; the area of a square is side × side.
formulae: Area (Rectangle) = l × b; Area (Square) = s × s
examples: Floor carpet problem: Area not carpeted = (5m × 4m floor) - (3m × 3m carpet) = 11 sq m.
Corner Bed Activity: Land 12m × 10m with four 4m-side flower beds in corners; remaining area = 120 - (4 × 16) = 56 sq m.
Tiling costs: A 500m × 200m plot costs Rs 8,000 at Rs 8 per hundred sq m.
Grove: A 100m × 50m grove can fit 200 trees if each needs 25 sq m.

concepts.title: Area of a Triangle
concepts.definition: A triangle formed by a diagonal of a rectangle is half of the rectangle's area.
formulae: Area (Triangle) = 1/2 × Area (Rectangle)
examples: A diagonal split shows two overlapping triangles of equal area.
In rectangle ABCD, triangle BAD is exactly half of ABCD.

concepts.title: Area and Perimeter Relationships
concepts.definition: Two figures can have the same area with different perimeters, or the same perimeter with different areas.
examples: Using 9 unit squares (Area 9): Perimeter is 12 (3×3 square) or 20 (1×9 rectangle).
Possible perimeters for area 24: 50 (1×24), 28 (2×12), 22 (3×8), 20 (4×6).
Comparison: Shape A (18 sq units) can have a larger perimeter than Shape B (20 sq units).

concepts.title: Practical Geometry (Tangrams and House Plans)
concepts.definition: Application of area/perimeter in spatial puzzles and architecture.
examples: Tangrams: The large square made of 7 pieces is 16 times the area of Shape C.
House plans: Charan's house (35ft × 30ft) and Sharan's house (42ft × 25ft) both have area 1050 sq ft.
Perimeter check: Sharan's perimeter (134ft) is larger than Charan's (130ft).
Area Maze: Finding missing values like side 5cm for a 16 sq cm / 30 sq cm region puzzle.

concepts.title: Circles and Compass Tools
concepts.definition: A circle is a curve formed by all points at a fixed distance (radius) from a fixed point (centre). A compass is the primary tool for constructing these curves by keeping the tip fixed and moving the pencil.
examples: Drawing a circle of radius 4 cm by opening the compass against a ruler.
Recreating Fig 8.1 artwork with precise ruler and compass use.

concepts.title: Geometrical Artwork
concepts.definition: Creating complex figures using combinations of circles, arcs, and straight lines.
examples: A Person: Finding where to place the tip of the compass for symmetric curves.
Wavy Wave: Recreating an 8 cm central line with 2 cm radius half-circles (Length AX = 4 cm).
Eyes: Drawing symmetrical upper and lower curves using trial and error for tip placement A and B.

concepts.title: Properties of Squares and Rectangles
concepts.definition: Squares and rectangles are 4-sided figures with specific constraints on side lengths and all angles equal to 90°.
formulae: Rectangle: Opposite sides equal (R1); All angles = 90° (R2)
Square: All sides equal (S1); All angles = 90° (S2)
examples: Valid Naming: Corners in order of travel (ABCD, BCDA, etc.; ABDC is invalid).
Rotated figures: Rotating a square piece of paper doesn't change its side lengths or 90° angles.

concepts.title: Step-by-Step Construction
concepts.definition: Constructing figures accurately using a ruler and compass to mark perpendiculars and transfer distances.
examples: Square (6 cm side): Constructing PQ and marking perpendiculars through P and Q.
Method 2: Using a compass instead of a ruler to mark point S on the perpendicular at exactly 6 cm from P.

concepts.title: Rectangular Explorations
concepts.definition: Exploring properties by moving points X and Y or subdividing rectangles into identical squares.
examples: Distance XY: Minimum when X is at A and Y is at B (XY = AB); farthest when X and Y are at opposite corners.
Dividing Rectangles: A rectangle of 12 cm × 4 cm can be divided into three 4 cm identical squares.
Puzzle: Sides of a rectangle that *cannot* be divided into two identical squares (e.g., 4 cm × 2.5 cm).

concepts.title: Properties of Diagonals
concepts.definition: The diagonals of a rectangle are equal in length and divide opposite angles into two parts.
formulae: Diagonals PR and QS: Length(PR) = Length(QS)
examples: Angle construction: Constructing a rectangle where a diagonal divides an angle into 60° and 30°.
Special case: In a square, a diagonal divides the 90° angles into two equal 45° parts.

concepts.title: Construction given Side and Diagonal
concepts.definition: Building a rectangle with one known side (e.g., 5 cm) and a diagonal (e.g., 7 cm) using arc intersections.
examples: Efficient Method: Draw a perpendicular to base DC at C; use compass from D with radius 7 cm to mark point B on the line.

concepts.title: Points Equidistant and Shape Symmetry
concepts.definition: Using arcs from two fixed points to find a third point that is equally distant from both.
examples: House peak 'A': Intersecting two 5 cm arcs from base points B and C to find 'A' exactly 5 cm from both.
Symmetry: A square with $8 cm sidelengths and 4 arcs bulging uniformly from each side.
Existence: A 4-sided figure with equal sides but not a square (Rhombus properties).

concepts.title: Multiples and Common Multiples
concepts.definition: Multiples of a number are values obtained by multiplying it by whole numbers (1, 2, 3...). Common multiples are those shared by two or more numbers.
examples: Idli-Vada Game: Multiples of 3 (idli), Multiples of 5 (vada), Common Multiples (idli-vada, e.g., 15, 30).
10th occurrence of idli-vada is at 150.
If played till 90: 'idli' said 30 times, 'vada' 18 times, 'idli-vada' 6 times.

concepts.title: Factors and Divisors
concepts.definition: Factors (or divisors) are numbers that divide a given number exactly, leaving zero remainder.
examples: Jump Jackpot: Jump sizes (factors) of 24 are 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24.
Common factors: Jump sizes that reach both 14 and 36 (e.g., 1 and 2).
Common factors of 20 and 28: 1, 2, 4.

concepts.title: Perfect Numbers
concepts.definition: A number for which the sum of all its factors is equal to twice the number.
formulae: Sum of factors = 2 × n
examples: The number 6: factors 1, 2, 3, 6 (Sum = 12 = 2 × 6).
The number 28: factors 1, 2, 4, 7, 14, 28 (Sum = 56 = 2 × 28).

concepts.title: Prime and Composite Numbers
concepts.definition: Prime numbers have exactly two factors (1 and itself). Composite numbers have more than two factors. The number 1 is neither prime nor composite.
examples: Arranging Figs: 7 figs can only form 1×7 rectangle (Prime); 12 figs can form 1×12, 2×6, 3×4 (Composite).
Sieve of Eratosthenes: A method developed ~2200 years ago to list primes up to 100 by crossing out multiples.

concepts.title: Special Primes and Patterns
concepts.definition: Exploring unique properties, differences, and digit-based relationships among primes.
examples: Smallest prime: 2 (the only even prime).
Twin primes: Pairs with difference 2 (e.g., 3 & 5, 17 & 19, 71 & 73).
Digit Swap: Pairs like 13 and 31 (both prime).
Consecutive composites: 90, 91, 92, 93, 94, 95, 96 (between 1 and 100).

concepts.title: Co-prime Numbers
concepts.definition: Two numbers are co-prime if they have no common factor other than 1.
formulae: Product Relationship: For co-prime pairs, the first common multiple is their product.
examples: Safe Treasure pairs: 4 and 15, 18 and 29.
Co-prime check: 18 and 35 are co-prime; 30 and 415 are not (common factor 5).
Thread Art: Pattern follows pegs vs gap relationship being co-prime.

concepts.title: Prime Factorisation
concepts.definition: Writing a number as a product of its prime factors. Every number > 1 has exactly one prime factorisation (Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic).
examples: Order: $198 = 2 × 3 × 3 × 11$ (Increasing order is standard).
Divisibility rule: If one number divides another, its prime factorisation is included in the larger number's (e.g., 168 / 12).

concepts.title: Divisibility Tests (2, 4, 5, 8, 10)
concepts.definition: Quick methods to check factor-hood without long division.
formulae: 10: Units digit is 0
5: Units digit is 0 or 5
2: Units digit is 0, 2, 4, 6, 8
4: Last two digits are divisible by 4
8: Last three digits are divisible by 8
examples: 14560: Divisible by 5 (ends in 0) and 8 (560 / 8 = 70).
Leap Year: Year divisible by 4 (e.g., 2024), except multiples of 100 (unless also 400).

concepts.title: Number Puzzles
concepts.definition: Logical application of prime/composite properties and divisibility.
examples: Who am I?: Number < 100, factors 3 & 5, digits differ by 1 (Ans: 45).
Special Numbers Box: Identifying a number as special for different reasons (e.g., in {9, 16, 25, 43}, 43 is prime, 9 is single-digit, 16 is even).
Prime Puzzle: Filling a grid with primes such that row/column products match targets (e.g., target 105 needs factors 3, 5, 7).
Product of 10,000 using non-zero units: $16 × 625$.
Smallest number multiple of all 1-10 except 7: 360.
Smallest number multiple of all 1-10: 2520.

concepts.title: Understanding Symmetry
concepts.definition: A quality of figures where parts repeat in a definite pattern, creating a balanced and beautiful appearance.
examples: Natural examples: Butterfly, flower petals.
Architectural examples: Taj Mahal, Gopuram, New Parliament Building.
Rangoli: Patterns that repeat upon rotation or reflection.

concepts.title: Line of Symmetry
concepts.definition: A line that cuts a figure into two parts (mirror halves) that exactly overlap when folded along that line.
formulae: Folding test: One half covers the other exactly.
examples: Triangle: A dotted line dividing a triangle into two identical halves.
Asymmetry: Clouds usually do not have a line of symmetry.

concepts.title: Multiple Lines of Symmetry
concepts.definition: A figure can have more than one axis of symmetry depending on its shape.
examples: Square: 4 lines of symmetry (Vertical, Horizontal, and both Diagonals).
Rectangle: 2 lines of symmetry (Vertical and Horizontal). Note: Diagonals of a non-square rectangle are NOT lines of symmetry.
Regular Polygons: n-sided regular polygon has n lines of symmetry (e.g., Hexagon has 6).

concepts.title: Reflection Symmetry
concepts.definition: A type of symmetry where one half of a figure is the mirror image of the other half.
formulae: Mapping: Point B reflects to A, C reflects to D across a vertical axis.
examples: Labeling: In a square ABCD, reflecting across a vertical line swaps B with A and C with D.

concepts.title: Generating Symmetric Shapes
concepts.definition: Creating symmetrical figures through simple activities like folding and cutting.
examples: Ink Blot Devils: Spilling ink on one half of a folded paper and pressing to create a symmetric blot.
Paper Folding/Cutting: Folding paper multiple times and making cuts to create intricate festive decorations.
Punching Game: Identifying folds by looking at the positions of punched holes on an unfolded sheet.

concepts.title: Rotational Symmetry
concepts.definition: A figure has rotational symmetry if it looks exactly the same after being rotated by some angle strictly between 0° and 360° about a fixed point (centre of rotation).
formulae: Angle of Symmetries: Multiples of the smallest angle of rotation.
examples: Paper Windmill: Looks the same after 90°, 180°, 270°, and 360° rotations.
Order of rotation: A square has rotational symmetry of order 4 (smallest angle 90°).

concepts.title: Radial Arms and Angles of Symmetry
concepts.definition: Determining rotational symmetry using arms radiating from a centre.
formulae: Smallest Angle = 360° / Number of radial arms (if equally distributed).
examples: 3 Radial Arms: Each angle must be 120° (360/3) to have rotational symmetry.
7 Radial Arms: Smallest angle is 360/7 ≈ 51 3/7°.

concepts.title: Symmetries of a Circle
concepts.definition: The circle is the most symmetric figure, possessing both infinite reflectional and rotational symmetries.
examples: Line Symmetry: Every diameter of a circle is an axis of symmetry.
Rotational Symmetry: Every angle (no matter how small) is an angle of symmetry for a circle.

concepts.title: Symmetry in Objects and Polygons
concepts.definition: Identifying lines and angles of symmetry in complex structures and standard polygons.
examples: New Parliament Building: 3 lines of reflection symmetry and 3 angles of rotational symmetry (120°, 240°, 360°).
Ashoka Chakra: 24 lines and 24 angles of symmetry.
Regular Hexagon: 6 lines and 6 angles of symmetry.
Koch Snowflake: Multiple lines and angles of symmetry depending on the sequence stage.

concepts.title: Introduction to Integers
concepts.definition: Numbers that extend to the left of zero on the number line, representing quantities less than zero.
examples: Counting numbers: 1, 2, 3, ...
Whole numbers: 0, 1, 2, 3, ...
Integers include zero and negative numbers.

concepts.title: Bela's Building of Fun Model
concepts.definition: A multistoried building used as a model to understand positive and negative numbers relative to the ground floor (zero).
formulae: Positive Numbers (+): Floors above ground.
Negative Numbers (-): Floors below ground.
Zero: Ground floor (Welcome Hall), neither positive nor negative.
examples: Pressing +2 reaches the Art Centre from 0.
Pressing +5 reaches the Sports Centre.
Pressing -1 reaches the Toy Store from 0.
Floor -2 is the Video Games shop and Floor -3 is the Cinema Centre.

concepts.title: Addition as Movement
concepts.definition: The process of tracking movement from a starting floor by adding lift button presses.
formulae: Starting Floor + Movement = Target Floor
examples: (+1) + (+2) = +3 (Food Court to Book Store).
(+2) + (-3) = -1 (Art Centre to Toy Store).
Inverse: (+3) + (-3) = 0 (Cancelling movement to return to ground floor).

concepts.title: Additive Inverse
concepts.definition: Two numbers whose sum is zero are called additive inverses of each other.
examples: Inverse of +4 is -4.
Inverse of -3 is +3.
Being at Floor -2 and pressing its inverse +2 takes you to floor 0.

concepts.title: Comparing Integers
concepts.definition: Determining which integer is larger or smaller based on their vertical or horizontal position.
formulae: Lower Floor < Higher Floor
Number line: Left < Right
examples: Compare +3 and +4: +3 < +4.
Compare -4 and -3: Floor -4 is lower than Floor -3, so -4 < -3.
All negative numbers are less than 0.

concepts.title: Subtraction as Finding Movement
concepts.definition: Finding the button press (movement) required to go from a starting floor to a target floor.
formulae: Target Floor - Starting Floor = Movement needed
examples: Target +5, Starting +2: (+5) - (+2) = +3 (Go 3 floors up).
Target -1, Starting -2: (-1) - (-2) = +1 (Go 1 floor up).
Target -2, Starting +2: (-2) - (+2) = -4 (Go 4 floors down).

concepts.title: Unmarked Number Line (UNL)
concepts.definition: Using a horizontal line with only zero marked to visualize and evaluate expressions with large numbers.
examples: Subtracting a negative number is the same as adding the corresponding positive: +2000 - (-200) = +2200.

concepts.title: The Token Model
concepts.definition: Using red (positive) and green (negative) tokens to perform arithmetic operations.
formulae: Zero Pair: One positive token and one negative token cancel each other.
examples: Addition: (+5) + (-3) = +2 (Remove 3 zero pairs, 2 positives remain).
Subtraction: (+5) - (+6) involves adding a zero pair to take away 6 positives, leaving -1.
Subtraction of negatives: (+4) - (-6) involves adding 6 zero pairs to take away 6 negatives, leaving 10 positives.

concepts.title: Applications of Integers
concepts.definition: Real-world scenarios where positive and negative numbers are essential for precision.
examples: Banking: Credits (Positive) vs Debits (Negative). A balance of -₹30 is possible.
Geography: Heights above sea level (Everest +8848 m) vs depths below (Mariana Trench -10994 m).
Temperature: Leh at -4°C relative to 0°C (freezing point of water).

concepts.title: Brahmagupta's Rules (628 CE)
concepts.definition: Historical rules for arithmetic operations on positive numbers, negative numbers, and zero.
formulae: Sum of two positives is positive.
Sum of two negatives is negative.
Sum of a number and its inverse is zero.
Subtracting zero from a number gives the same number.
examples: -5 + 3 = -2 (Rule 3: place sign of greater number).
Subtracting a negative is same as adding positive: 2 - (-3) = 2 + 3 (Rule 3 for subtraction).

concepts.title: History of Integers
concepts.definition: The development of integers across different cultures and eras.
examples: Jiuzhang Suanshu (China): Red/black rods for accounting.
Bakshali Manuscript (India): Negative symbol after the number.
Arthashastra (Kautilya): Extensive use of credit/debit concepts.
European Acceptance: Negative numbers were called 'absurd' by Lazare Carnot in the 18th century.

concepts.title: Games and Puzzles
concepts.definition: Learning integer operations through logical challenges and games.
examples: Snake & Ladders: Played with two dice (+1 to +6 and -1 to -6), where players move based on the sum or difference.
Hollow Integer Grid: Numbers in rows and columns must add up to a specific 'border sum'.
Sum Trick Grid: A magic property where choosing numbers from different rows/columns always yields the same total.
